title: Polymarket's viral videos showed people winning big, but the bets were fake
gist: A Wall Street Journal investigation revealed that Polymarket paid dozens of social media creators to film fake bets on dummy websites, generating 140 million views across TikTok, YouTube, and Instagram while hiding that creators were paid and that bets were simulated—a deceptive marketing campaign targeting US users despite Polymarket being barred from operating its main exchange in the US since a 2022 CFTC enforcement action.

themes.theme: Deceptive marketing campaign scale and mechanics

insights.claim: Polymarket paid social media creators $2,000-$3,000 per month to film fake bets on dummy websites (including a misspelled clone, poiymarket.com), generating 140 million views across TikTok, YouTube, and Instagram while requiring creators to hide their paid status and avoid disclosing the bets were simulated.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: 140 million views of deceptive content targeting US retail users is a material FTC and CFTC regulatory exposure event; the campaign directly contradicts Polymarket's claim to be 'committed to maintaining accurate, fair, and transparent markets.'
insights.quote: Polymarket has flooded social media with videos like [George] Makihara's, which appear genuine at first glance. In reality, Polymarket built near-perfect copies of its website, then instructed creators to make simulated trades on those dummy sites and hide that they were being paid by Polymarket.

insights.claim: The WSJ reviewed 1,105 videos from 10 creators and identified fake bets totaling $1.9 million in simulated winnings, including 118 videos of creators reacting to fake headlines showing ~$900,000 in wins that would in reality have lost over $166,000.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: The financial misrepresentation—showing $900K in wins on positions that lost $166K—meets the FTC's deceptive advertising standard; this is more than influencer non-disclosure, it is materially false financial performance advertising.
insights.quote: Those 118 videos showed creators winning almost $900,000, but the bets in reality would have lost over $166,000.

insights.claim: Polymarket specifically designed its campaign to target US residents—paying creators only when at least 60 percent of their viewers were in the United States—despite Polymarket's main platform being legally barred from US users since a 2022 CFTC enforcement action.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Deliberately targeting a jurisdiction where your core product is barred is an aggravating factor in any regulatory proceeding; this is likely to complicate Polymarket's pending application to the CFTC for re-authorization in the US.
insights.quote: The promotion reportedly targeted US residents by paying creators only when at least 60 percent of their viewers were in the United States.

themes.theme: Regulatory status and CFTC re-authorization risk

insights.claim: Polymarket has been barred from operating its main exchange in the US since 2022 when the CFTC determined it was running an illegally unregistered exchange; it currently offers only a limited US-regulated version through its Polymarket US app, acquired via the purchase of CFTC-licensed QCX.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Polymarket's US revenue is structurally capped by its regulatory status; the deceptive campaign directly undermines its CFTC re-authorization prospects and could result in the Polymarket US app license being revisited.

insights.claim: Both Polymarket and its rival Kalshi have Donald Trump Jr. as an advisor, and a Trump Jr.-backed venture capital firm invested in Polymarket; the Trump administration has actively tried to limit prediction market regulation and the CFTC has been suing states that impose strict rules on prediction markets.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Political connections may provide regulatory cover for Polymarket in the near term, but create binary risk if political winds shift or the FTC acts independently of CFTC on deceptive advertising grounds.
insights.quote: Polymarket and its rival Kalshi both have Donald Trump Jr. as an advisor, and a Trump Jr.-backed venture capital firm invested in Polymarket.

insights.claim: The Trump administration is actively using the CFTC to sue states that try to impose strict prediction market rules, centralizing regulatory authority under a more permissive federal regime—a structural benefit for Polymarket and Kalshi regardless of the deceptive marketing scandal.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Federal pre-emption of state prediction market rules is a regulatory tailwind for the sector; the deceptive marketing issue is a headwind that may be contained at the federal level given current administration posture.

themes.theme: Reputation and trust risk for prediction markets

insights.claim: Polymarket's response to the WSJ investigation was to announce a 'comprehensive audit of active promotional content' without addressing specific findings—a non-denial that did not dispute the core fakery allegations, potentially signaling awareness of legal exposure.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Non-specific audit responses to specific documented allegations often precede enforcement settlements; monitor for FTC investigation initiation or Polymarket CFTC application withdrawal as follow-on signals.
insights.quote: We are conducting a comprehensive audit of active promotional content to ensure it complies with our standards, as well as applicable regulatory and legal disclosure requirements.

insights.claim: Polymarket removed the fake poiymarket.com clone site immediately after the WSJ contacted the company for comment—implicit confirmation that the company was aware of and controlled the deceptive infrastructure.
insights.speaker: Jon Brodkin
insights.so_what: Same-day takedown after press inquiry is strong evidence of knowledge and intent, not third-party contractor behavior; this distinguishes the case from typical influencer non-disclosure and elevates it toward platform liability.
insights.quote: The 'poiymarket' website was taken down after the Journal reached out to Polymarket for comment.
